SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main technical claims are clearly stated and supported by appropriate derivations: the Gaussian log-likelihood scoring (Eq. 1), relation parameterization as mean+covariance, and compositionality via sum of means and variances are mathematically consistent for diagonal Gaussians. The training objectives (ranking loss) and the QA model (LSTM-based relation prediction, Eq. 6–7) are sensible and implemented with reasonable baselines (TransE variants). However, the experiments use a relatively small, manually templated dataset, assume an oracle entity recognizer, and report no statistical significance tests; these limit the strength of empirical support and leave some practical generalization questions open (also diagonal covariance restricts expressivity).

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper adapts Gaussian embeddings/attentions to the problem of knowledge-base embedding and question answering, including natural usage for uncertainty propagation along relation chains and conjunction via product of Gaussians — a non-trivial combination not previously applied in this exact setting. Prior works (e.g., Vilnis & McCallum on Gaussian embeddings, TransE, compositional training) are acknowledged and the paper clearly positions its contributions as an extension that unifies attention, compositional traversal and conjunction. The ideas are novel enough to be of interest, though they build on several established components.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized with a logical flow (introduction, model, QA, experiments, related work, conclusion) and equations and notation are consistently presented (e.g., Eq. 1–7). Key assumptions (oracle entity recognizer, diagonal covariance) are explicitly stated and limitations are discussed in the experiments/appendix. Some parts (figures/tables referenced in the text) were not visible in the provided excerpt and the dataset construction relied on manual templates, but overall the exposition is clear and understandable.
